Autonomous Hardware

Intro
The autonomous mission intends to rely on ROS2, Gazebo, Ardupilot/rover, and some other smaller packages. The 2026 capstone team felt that the integration of ROS2 and Gazebo would not be ready for our competition, so we found a way to autonomously operate the rover, as if it were a drone. We use a branch of Arudupilot, called Ardurover, which allows us to input a GPS coordinate, collect sensor and camera output, as well as integrate ROS2 for other system controls. Ardurover is the autopilot firmware that is installed on the flight controller, while mission planner is the software that we use to interact with the autopilot firmware.

Why
The Ardupilot approach was intended to simplify the system controls, navigation, and integration portions of the mission. Ardupilot can be implemented to work with ROS2 and Gazebo for cost mapping, and various other autonomous system controls.

Equipment
Flight controller - Pixhawk 6C (Figure 1)
GPS - M10 GPS (Figure 2)

  • There are multiple GPS units. The primary (required) one features a 10 pin connector. All GPS units with a \~6 pin connector are secondary (not required) GPS units.

Motor Breakout Board - PWM EXT Board (Figure 3)
Software - Mission Planner (recommend using Windows 11)

First Time Setup
If you are setting up a new computer please start at step 1., If you are setting up a new flight controller, please skip to step #####

  1. Download Mission Planner (Recommend using Windows 11)
  2. Follow the Mission Planner installation instructions (also available here on Github)
  3. Install all of the necessary drivers, software packages, and log into the Altitude Angel. The software packages should prompt you for their installation.
  4. Once you have completed all of the set up, you should be greeted with a screen that is relatively similar to Figure 4 (map location, and vehicle information will vary). If your screen is different, or you have errors at this stage, please troubleshoot before continuing.
  5. Connect the flight controller to your computer via the white USB-C cable.
  6. Plug in the primary (10 pin) GPS, and any secondary (6 pin) GPS units.
  7. In the top right corner of Mission Planner, set the Communications Port (COM) to AUTO. All other boxes should grey out. Click connect. In Figure 5 the green boxes highlight the tab’s location.
